intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to bright, cheerful, low-detail illustrations than photorealistic food imagery
facade.label: Colorful flat icons
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into playful, abstracted food visuals; avoid appetizing food porn
facade.rejectImplies: Consider photo-based recipe steps or more sophisticated visual treatment

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to bright, cheerful, low-detail illustrations than photorealistic food imagery
evidence.implication: Lean into playful, abstracted food visuals; avoid appetizing food porn

facade.hypothesis: A voice that mocks the cooking process and validates user frustration through humor will feel more relatable and motivating than neutral instruction
facade.label: Sarcastic & sassy
facade.acceptImplies: User gravitates toward irreverent, joke-filled copy that treats cooking frustration as shared experience rather than problem to solve seriously
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers straightforward, respectful guidance without attitude—voice should be helpful and kind, not comedic or edgy

facade.hypothesis: For people who hate cooking, visual ingredient selection (photo grid) reduces friction more than text-based search or lists
facade.label: Pick ingredients by photo
facade.acceptImplies: Users prefer seeing ingredient images over reading names or typing — design should prioritize photo browsing, gallery views, and visual ingredient cards
facade.rejectImplies: Text-based ingredient input (search, autocomplete, lists) is sufficient or preferable — photo galleries add unnecessary bulk and cognitive load

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking benefits from bright, playful UI that makes the experience feel less like a chore and more like a game or reward.
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool, energetic vs calm
facade.label: Cheerful, colorful buttons
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward energetic, warm, playful mood—wants the app to feel encouraging and fun rather than clinical or intimidating.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers a more neutral, calm, or understated aesthetic—wants the app to feel effortless and low-pressure rather than enthusiastic or in-your-face.

facade.hypothesis: For recipe-averse users, showing one full recipe at a time (large, spacious, minimal competing options) reduces cognitive load and decision paralysis vs. browsing a list
facade.axisTargeted: Density & Focus (dense grid vs. spacious single-item view)
facade.label: One big recipe card
facade.acceptImplies: User leans toward distraction-free, guided experience—design as progressive disclosure, large touch targets, one-thing-at-a-time flow
facade.rejectImplies: User wants to scan & compare multiple options at once—design as overview-first, list or grid layout, quick filtering and side-by-side contrast

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want recipes to appear immediately without waiting for animations, avoiding the friction of transitions
facade.axisTargeted: MOTION AND BEHAVIOR — speed and immediacy of information reveal
facade.label: Instant snap vs smooth slide
facade.acceptImplies: Recipe app should prioritize snappy, instant UI transitions; animations feel like unnecessary overhead for busy, impatient users
facade.rejectImplies: Users actually want fluid, animated transitions between recipe steps and ingredient lists; motion makes the experience feel less tedious and more engaging

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user prioritizes time estimates and difficulty ratings vs. nutritional data and ingredient sourcing details
Whether user wants smooth, animated guidance through recipes vs. instant, snappy transitions and minimal loading states

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer clean, understated interfaces without distracting food imagery or visual clutter
facade.label: Dark & minimal
facade.acceptImplies: Build with dark mode as default; use negative space and typography over decorative elements; reduce visual noise
facade.rejectImplies: Bright, saturated, decorated interfaces work better; users want visual richness and detail despite hating cooking itself

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so simple, you'll actually make them

Filter by time
Today's picks
Buttered Toast with Egg
⏱ 4 min
🛒 3 items
Pasta Aglio e Olio
⏱ 8 min
🛒 4 items
Quesadilla with Cheese
⏱ 5 min
🛒 2 items
